title: Tanking a cellar wall with waterproofing slurry
subtitle: Apply a cementitious slurry coat to stop water ingress through masonry
excerpt: How to apply a cementitious waterproofing slurry (tanking compound) to a cellar or basement wall to prevent water ingress. Covers surface preparation, mixing the slurry, application sequence, and the critical corner and floor-to-wall joint detail.
sourceNotes: British Standard BS 8102: Code of practice for the protection of below-ground structures against water from the ground. Property Care Association (PCA) guidance on basement waterproofing. Fosroc and Sika application guidance (referenced, not reproduced).

glossaryTerms.definition: A powder-and-liquid two-part or powder-only system that, when mixed and applied to masonry, penetrates and chemically reacts with the cement matrix to block capillaries and pores. Unlike a paint-on membrane, it bonds to the substrate and withstands positive (water pressing from outside) and negative (water pressing from inside) water pressure. Sold under trade names including Sika 1, Fosroc Renderoc, and similar branded products.

Positive water pressure is groundwater pressing from the outside of a wall inward — the most common cellar waterproofing scenario. Negative water pressure is the opposite: water inside a structure trying to escape outward. Cementitious slurry is rated for both; sheet membranes and paint-on coatings typically resist only positive pressure and can detach under negative pressure.

content.text: stops water ingress through brick or block basement walls. Walls must be damp but not actively leaking. If water flows through a crack, plug it first with rapid-setting hydraulic cement. The slurry bonds to the masonry and resists both
. The critical rule is continuity: any gap in the coating, especially at the floor-to-wall joint, lets water pass.

Cementitious waterproofing powder compound (calculate coverage from the manufacturer's data sheet; typically 1.5 to 2 kg per square metre per coat, applied in two coats). Mixing bucket and a slow-speed drill with mixing paddle. A stiff-bristle brush or a masonry brush for application. A trowel for key-coat and floor-to-wall fillet. Rapid-setting hydraulic cement for any active cracks. Protective gloves and goggles.

Rake out any loose mortar from brickwork joints to a depth of 10 mm using the SDS drill with a chisel bit or a cold chisel and hammer. Remove all paint, efflorescence (white salt deposits), and loose render. Active cracks (cracks from which water is actively flowing) must be plugged with rapid-setting hydraulic cement before proceeding: press the cement firmly into the crack and hold it for 3 to 4 minutes until it sets. The wall should be damp but not wet when the slurry is applied: dry masonry absorbs the slurry too fast; running wet prevents adhesion.

Before applying the slurry to the main wall, form a coved fillet at the floor-to-wall junction using the same waterproofing compound mixed to a stiffer consistency. Press a 50 mm triangular fillet of compound into the corner using the trowel, filling the right-angle junction with a curved or chamfered profile. This eliminates the square internal corner, which is a stress point and a difficult area to achieve continuous coverage over. Allow the fillet to stiffen before applying the main wall coats.
Step 3, apply the first slurry coat

Mix the waterproofing compound to a thick slurry consistency (the consistency of thick yogurt) according to the data sheet ratios; do not guess or thin. Apply the first coat with the stiff brush in a vigorous scrubbing motion, working the material into the masonry pores and mortar joints. Apply from the floor-to-wall fillet upward, maintaining a continuous wet coat across the surface. Include the floor if the brief specifies a fully tanked box; a wall-only treatment is sometimes appropriate if the floor is poured concrete without cracks. The first coat is the key coat: its job is to penetrate and key, not to look smooth. Typical wet film thickness is 1 to 2 mm.

Apply the second coat once the first has set to a firm consistency; not fully cured, but firm enough to resist indentation. On most products this is 3 to 6 hours. The second coat is applied in a crosshatch direction to the first to maximise coverage and eliminate any pinholes. Mix fresh material for this coat. The finished system should be pinhole-free; any pinhole is a potential water path. Allow the full cure time (usually 3 to 7 days depending on the product) before any finishes are applied.

attrs.symptom: Water still seeping through the wall after tanking
attrs.cause: Active cracks were not plugged before tanking, or coverage was too thin at a pinhole.
attrs.fix: Identify the entry point by watching the wall during rain or after a prolonged wet period. Plug the entry point with hydraulic cement, then apply a localised repair coat of the tanking compound over the plugged area.

attrs.symptom: Tanking coat delaminating from the wall
attrs.cause: Wall surface was too smooth (painted) or too dry when the slurry was applied.
attrs.fix: Remove all delaminating material. Re-prepare the substrate — remove all paint and prime with a SBR bonding agent. Re-apply the tanking system.
